name: temporal-python-pro
description: Master Temporal workflow orchestration with Python SDK. Implements
capabilities: Guide worker configuration with proper task queue setup and graceful shutdown
Design workflow patterns using @workflow.defn and @workflow.run decorators
Implement activities with sync/async execution models and ThreadPoolExecutor
Configure retry policies, timeouts, and error handling strategies
Create signal and query handlers for external workflow interaction
Set up time-skipping test environments for fast workflow validation

useCases.title: Microservices Orchestration
useCases.description: Coordinate distributed transactions across multiple services using saga patterns with compensation logic.

useCases.targetUser: Systems architects designing business process automation
useCases.title: Long-Running Business Processes
useCases.description: Implement workflows that span hours to years with automatic state persistence and recovery.

useCases.targetUser: Data engineers creating ETL and ML pipelines
useCases.title: Data Processing Pipelines
useCases.description: Build multi-stage data transformation workflows with parallel batch processing and error handling.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Workflow Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me create a basic Temporal workflow in Python that processes user orders. I need to validate the order, charge payment, and send a confirmation email. Show me the workflow definition and activity implementations.

promptTemplates.title: Retry Policy Configuration
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to configure retry policies for an activity that calls an external API with rate limits. The API allows 100 requests per minute and may return 429 errors. What retry policy settings should I use?

promptTemplates.title: Signal Handler Implementation
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I implement a signal handler that allows users to cancel a running workflow? Show me how to validate the signal, handle cancellation gracefully, and emit compensation activities.

promptTemplates.title: Production Testing Strategy
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to test a workflow that spans 30 days using time-skipping. Create a test that validates the workflow completes all activities in the correct order and handles timeout scenarios.

bestPractices: Keep workflows focused on orchestration logic; move all external calls to activities
Use workflow.now() and workflow.random() instead of datetime and random modules for determinism
Implement idempotent activities to ensure safe retries on transient failures
Configure appropriate timeouts: schedule_to_close for total duration, start_to_close for single attempts
Use time-skipping test environment for fast feedback on long-running workflows
antiPatterns: Using datetime.now() or random.random() directly in workflow code breaks determinism
Making direct API calls from workflows instead of using activities
Not handling ActivityError in workflows, losing ability to implement compensation
Blocking the async event loop with synchronous code in async activities
Exceeding 2MB payload size limits by passing large data between activities

faq.question: What is the difference between a workflow and an activity in Temporal?
faq.answer: Workflows define orchestration logic and must be deterministic. Activities perform external operations like API calls or database queries. Workflows call activities, which handle the actual work.

faq.question: How do I test workflows that run for days or weeks?
faq.answer: Use the WorkflowEnvironment with time-skipping enabled. This executes workflow.sleep() calls instantly, allowing you to test month-long workflows in seconds during unit tests.

faq.question: Can I update workflow code after deployment?
faq.answer: Yes, but you must maintain determinism for existing running workflows. Use workflow.get_version() to handle code changes gracefully and test with replay against production histories.

faq.question: What happens when an activity fails?
faq.answer: Temporal automatically retries based on your RetryPolicy configuration. You can specify initial interval, backoff coefficient, maximum attempts, and mark certain error types as non-retryable.

faq.question: How do signals differ from queries?
faq.answer: Signals allow external systems to send events that modify workflow state. Queries provide read-only access to workflow state for monitoring. Signals can change state; queries cannot.

faq.question: What execution model should I use for activities?
faq.answer: Use async activities for non-blocking I/O, ThreadPoolExecutor for blocking sync I/O, and ProcessPoolExecutor for CPU-intensive tasks. Never block the async event loop.
